1: Elijah’s drought announcement — ḥay YHWH ʾelōhê yiśrāʾēl... ʾim yihyeh haššānîm hāʾēlleh ṭal ûmāṭār kî ʾim lᵉpî dᵉḇārî — ‘as YHWH the God of Israel lives, there shall be neither dew nor rain these years, except by my word’ — is a direct challenge to Baal’s claimed domain. In Canaanite religious cosmology, Baal (baʿal, ‘lord’) was the storm deity who controlled rain, thunder, and agricultural fertility. The Ugaritic Baal Cycle (tablets from Ras Shamra, ancient Ugarit, ca. 14th-13th century BCE; KTU 1.1-6) depicts Baal’s ongoing contest with Mot (death) for control of the rain: when Mot defeats Baal, the land dries; when Baal defeats Mot, the rains return. Baal’s enemies taunt him in the Cycle: ‘Baal has no house like the other gods.’ The drought announced by Elijah strips Baal of his one undisputed domain and assigns control of the rain to YHWH through his prophet’s spoken word alone — without the elaborate ritual combat of the Baal Cycle. The irony is compounded: the announcement is made to Ahab, whose wife Jezebel is the daughter of Ethbaal king of Sidon (ʾeṯbaʿal, ‘with Baal’) — the king whose very name declares Baal devotion. The drought is YHWH’s challenge delivered to Baal’s champion in Baal’s heartland.
9: YHWH’s command sending Elijah to Zarephath — qûm lēḵ ṣārᵉpatāh ʾăšer lᵉṣîḏôn, ‘arise, go to Zarephath, which belongs to Sidon’ — is geographically and theologically loaded. Zarephath (ṣārᵉpat, modern Sarafand in Lebanon) is a Phoenician coastal town between Tyre and Sidon, explicitly identified as Sidonian territory. Sidon was the home city of Jezebel’s father Ethbaal, the center of Phoenician Baal religion, and the source of the Baal/Asherah worship that the Omride-Jezebel axis had imported into Israel. Elijah is sent not to an Israelite widow but to a Gentile widow in the heartland of Baal worship — in the territory of the very deity whose rain-domain he had challenged, and whose leading human patron had married into the Israelite royal house. This mission embodies the theological principle that YHWH’s provision and presence are not bounded by covenant geography. Luke 4:26 applies this location explicitly in Jesus’s Nazareth sermon: ‘there were many widows in Israel in the days of Elijah... and Elijah was sent to none of them but only to Zarephath, in the land of Sidon, to a woman who was a widow’ — identifying Elijah’s Zarephath mission as the OT precedent for divine grace operating outside Israel’s borders, and provoking the congregation’s rejection of Jesus by the same logic: the grace that went to a Sidonian widow is now going to those outside the Nazareth congregation’s expectation.
2: The word of YHWH to Elijah: 'Depart from here and turn eastward and hide yourself by the brook Cherith, which is east of the Jordan.' The brook Cherith (naḥal kᵉrît) is not identified with certainty; the traditional location is Wadi Yabis in Transjordan, east of the Jordan. The command to hide (hissātēr) suggests that Elijah's safety after announcing the drought requires concealment from Ahab's court, which will search for him (1 Kings 18:10: 'Ahab has sent to seek you in every nation and kingdom').
3: The divine provision at Cherith: 'I have commanded the ravens to feed you there.' The verb ṣiwwîtî (I have commanded) is applied to the ravens — YHWH's sovereignty extends to directing wild birds. The LXX translators faced a problem with ʿōrᵉḇîm: the Hebrew is identical to the word for 'ravens' and also to the gentilc 'Arabians.' The LXX reads 'ravens' (korakes), following the MT. The miraculous feeding of the prophet by birds reverses the general pattern of Elijah announcing famine: the prophet himself is fed supernaturally while the land goes without rain.
4: YHWH's provision promise: 'You shall drink from the brook, and I have commanded the ravens to feed you there.' The double provision of water (from Cherith) and food (from ravens) addresses both survival needs. The narrative will show the water source fail first (v7) before the food source is shifted to the Zarephath widow — the provision adapts to the circumstances without interrupting the divine supply.
5: Elijah's obedience: 'He went and did according to the word of the LORD. He went and lived by the brook Cherith that is east of the Jordan.' The simple obedience formula — 'he went and did according to the word of YHWH' — is the characteristic mark of the faithful prophet in Deuteronomistic narrative. Elijah's going without question or condition establishes his prophetic character from the outset; his faithfulness will be tested later (1 Kings 19:4) but is established first.
6: The raven provision fulfilled: 'The ravens brought him bread and meat in the morning, and bread and meat in the evening, and he drank from the brook.' The morning-evening feeding pattern echoes the manna provision in the wilderness (Exodus 16:12-13: quail in the evening, manna in the morning). Elijah's Cherith episode is a miniature wilderness sojourn: divine provision in a desolate place, preparing the prophet for his mission. The miraculous feed sustains him for the drought's duration at Cherith.
7: The brook Cherith dried up because there was no rain in the land. The cause of the brook's failure — the very drought Elijah announced — dries up his own provision. The divine provision is not immune to the judgment it announced; Elijah experiences the drought he proclaimed. This provides the narrative occasion for the next stage: the move to Zarephath. The progressive nature of the drought (brook drying up) gives the narrative temporal development.
8: The word of the LORD came to Elijah again: the repeated prophetic formula signals a new divine directive following the exhaustion of the Cherith provision. The second directive moves the prophet from Israel's territory entirely to Phoenicia — Zarephath belongs to Sidon, Jezebel's homeland. The irony is striking: Elijah is sent to the very country whose Baal-worship Jezebel is importing into Israel, to be fed by a gentile widow.
10: Elijah came to the gate of the city and behold a widow was there gathering sticks. The gate as the commercial and social hub of ancient cities: the widow gathering sticks at the gate for fuel is in the most public possible location, which makes her accessible. The gate (šaʿar) is also where legal matters were transacted, where the poor came to beg, and where commerce was conducted — the widow at the gate is in the expected place of the destitute seeking help.
11: Elijah's first request: water. 'Please bring me a little water in a vessel, that I may drink.' The initial request for water — the most basic provision — is followed immediately by the second request for bread while she is already going (v12). The doubling of requests escalates the demand: water first, then food, from a widow who has 'nothing' (v12). The social position of the widow in the ancient Near East: without a husband or adult son, she had no male protector, no claim on common resources, and was dependent on charity.
12: The widow's response establishes her desperate condition: 'As YHWH your God lives, I have nothing baked, only a handful of flour in a jar and a little oil in a jug.' The oath by YHWH — the Israelite deity — from a Phoenician woman is striking; she may have had contact with Israelite religious culture through trade or travel, or this is the narrative's way of identifying her as a God-fearer. The specific provisions named — kad qāmāḥ (jar of flour) and ṣappaḥat šemen (cruse of oil) — give the account precise detail of subsistence-level food supply.
13: Elijah's counter-instruction: 'Do not fear. Go and do as you have said, but first make me a small cake from it and bring it to me, and afterward make something for yourself and your son.' The 'do not fear' (ʾal-tîrᵉʾî) formula typically introduces a divine reassurance; here it comes from the prophet. The instruction to feed the prophet first — before the widow feeds herself and her son — is the test of faith that precedes the miracle. The pattern: obedience before provision, not provision as reward for prior faith.
14: The miracle promise: 'For thus says YHWH the God of Israel: The jar of flour shall not be spent, and the jug of oil shall not be empty, until the day that YHWH sends rain upon the earth.' The specific miracle — inexhaustible small provisions — rather than a dramatic one-time provision, sustains through the duration of the drought. The formula 'YHWH the God of Israel' on a prophet's lips in Phoenicia is the signature of the mission to demonstrate YHWH's sovereignty over Baal's own territory.
15: She went and did as Elijah said — the obedience formula applied to the widow. The extended provision: 'She and he and her household ate for many days.' The widow's obedience in giving the prophet first brings the extended provision. The household (hû wayhî wᵉḇêtāh — he and she and her household) are all sustained. The miracle persists beyond the initial feeding: 'many days' (yāmîm rabbîm) indicates the ongoing duration of the supernatural supply.
16: The fulfillment confirmation: 'The jar of flour was not spent, neither did the jug of oil become empty, according to the word of YHWH that he spoke by Elijah.' The standard fulfillment formula confirms the prophetic word. The ongoing miracle of inexhaustible provisions has a loose parallel in the Elisha narrative (2 Kings 4:1-7, the widow's oil that fills vessels). Both accounts emphasize that YHWH's provision through prophetic agents operates without rational explanation — the jar and jug simply do not empty.
17: After this the son of the woman, the mistress of the house, became sick; his illness was so severe that there was no breath left in him. The death of the widow's son after the miraculous provision is a second crisis: the miracle that sustained life is followed by death. The Hebrew wayyᵉhî ʾaḥᵃrê haddᵉḇārîm hāʾēlleh ('after these things') is a formula for a narrative crisis following a narrative resolution. The child's breath (nᵉšāmāh) ceasing is the standard idiom for death (cf. Genesis 2:7 where YHWH breathes nišmat ḥayyîm into the man).
18: The widow's accusation: 'What have you against me, O man of God? You have come to me to bring my sin to remembrance and to cause the death of my son!' The theology of the accusation: in the ancient Near East, proximity to a holy person could bring divine punishment for previously unaddressed sins. The widow interprets her son's death as divine punishment for sin (ʿāwôn) that Elijah's presence has 'brought to remembrance' before God. The accusation is not directed at Elijah personally but at the God whose presence Elijah represents.
19: Elijah's response: 'Give me your son.' He takes the child from her bosom and carries him to the upper room where he lodged and lays him on his own bed. The upper room (ʿăliyyāh) was a guest room or private space above the main living area in ancient Israelite houses — the same term for the room where Elisha will raise the Shunammite woman's son (2 Kings 4:10-11, 21). The prophet takes the child into his private space for the healing — away from public view, in the intimate sphere where divine action can occur without audience.
20: Elijah's prayer of lament: 'O YHWH my God, have you brought calamity even upon the widow with whom I sojourn, by killing her son?' The prophet's prayer of lament directed at God — the Psalmic tradition of the lament prayer that accuses God of wrongdoing (hăgam ʿal-hāʾalmānāh, also upon the widow?) — is the model of prophetic intercession. Moses similarly accused God after the golden calf (Exodus 32:11-13); the prophets' ability to address God's apparent injustice reflects the intimacy of covenant relationship.
21: Elijah stretched himself on the child three times and cried to YHWH. The physical contact — stretching (wạyyitmōḏaḏ) the prophet's body on the child — is a gesture whose purpose is uncertain: symbolic identification of the living with the dead, or physical warming (proposed by rationalistic interpreters), or simply the physical gesture accompanying the prayer. Three repetitions establish the completeness of the act. A parallel in 2 Kings 4:34-35 has Elisha performing a similar stretching-action over the Shunammite's son.
22: YHWH heard the voice of Elijah and the soul of the child came into him again, and he revived. The resurrection formula: wayyāšāḇ nepeš hayyeledd ʿal-qirbo, the nepeš (life-breath, soul) of the child returned into him. The nepeš as the living principle that departs at death and can be restored by divine action is the OT's understanding of the human person as an animated body — the nepeš is not immortal by nature but given and sustained by YHWH. The restoration of the nepeš is a reversal of the death-process initiated in v17.
23: Elijah took the child and brought him down from the upper room into the house and gave him to his mother. The physical restoration — bringing the child from the prophet's private space back into the household — is the completion of the miracle. The child is returned to the mother as the intended recipient of the miracle. The movement from the upper room (space of death and prophetic action) to the house (space of family and ordinary life) is the spatial enactment of the child's restoration.
24: The widow's confession: 'Now I know that you are a man of God and that the word of YHWH in your mouth is truth.' The resurrection miracle produces the theological confession. The designation ʾîš hāʾelōhîm (man of God) is confirmed by the resurrection; the truth (ʾemet) of YHWH's word through his prophet is established by the act. The trajectory: drought announcement (v1), miraculous provision (vv14-16), resurrection (vv17-23), confession (v24). The Elijah cycle establishes the prophet's credentials through progressive demonstrations of YHWH's power through him.
